text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

Alex Morgan, DOB: 21-07-2004, Hospital No: U914735 NHS No. 3849201765  
Flat 3B, 42 Gower Street, London, WC1E 6DP 

Summary/ Diagnoses: History of seizures of unclear classification with patient-reported stress sensitivity; no confirmed structural brain lesion; normal neurological examination today apart from mild post-ictal fatigue features by history; sleep restriction related to upcoming university examinations. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily.  
Clobazam 5 mg as required at bedtime during high-stress periods (patient-reported intermittent use).  
Vitamin D3, Multivitamin.  

I reviewed Alex Morgan in clinic today. They attended alone, as their family is abroad and contact is mainly by phone. They are a university student currently preparing for end-of-term examinations and have been studying for prolonged periods most days. They report reduced sleep on weekdays (approximately 5–6 hours) and higher caffeine intake than usual. Appetite is variable but weight has been stable by their account. There is no history of head injury, CNS infection, or febrile convulsions. There has been no alcohol or recreational drug use. They describe increased stress around deadlines and a tendency to postpone breaks during long study sessions. 

Seizures: The history today is primarily patient-reported, as no seizure diary was brought to clinic. Convulsions seizures have been reported only twice in the last year. focal tonic with retained awareness are experienced two - six times daily, sometimes in clusters. These brief events are described as a sudden right arm and facial tightness with jaw clenching and a pull of the right shoulder, lasting 10–30 seconds, without loss of awareness, sometimes occurring several times across a few hours on days of poor sleep or high stress. They report a short post-event period of mental slowing but can continue tasks. No tongue biting or urinary incontinence has been reported outside of the two convulsive events, which occurred several months apart, both in the early morning after nights of heavy study. There have been no recent emergency department attendances. 

Examination: Pulse 68 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg. Afebrile. Neurological examination normal: cranial nerves intact, no focal weakness, coordination normal, gait steady. No photoparoxysmal sensitivity on casual exposure. 

Investigations: Prior routine EEG one year ago reportedly normal (patient-provided summary; formal report not available today). No MRI on record; patient unsure if one was ever performed. No recent anti-seizure medication levels or bone profile in the notes. 

Impact and safety: Episodes are more frequent during intensive revision periods, and the patient has adapted by taking short pauses after clusters. They do not drive. They live in university accommodation with flatmates aware of their condition. No water-based sports and showers preferred over baths by their account. 

Plan:  
- Advise maintenance of a detailed seizure and trigger diary (including sleep duration, caffeine intake, and study hours) and to bring this to next review.  
- No immediate change to current anti-seizure medication regimen based on today’s history and examination; reinforce adherence and stable dosing times.  
- Request GP to arrange blood tests: U&Es, LFTs, FBC, vitamin D, and bone profile within the next 4 weeks; please forward results to our clinic.  
- Provide patient with information on sleep hygiene, caffeine moderation, and regular breaks during revision, as discussed.  
- Consider MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and sleep-deprived EEG if seizure frequency persists at current levels despite optimisation of sleep; will revisit at next appointment once diary data available.  
- University support letter offered confirming medical condition and potential impact around examinations; patient to contact disability support services.  
- Safety counselling reiterated regarding bathing, heights, and late-night solitary study in unfamiliar settings.  

Follow-up: Review in 3 months or earlier if convulsive events recur or frequency escalates. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: focal tonic with retained awareness are experienced two - six times daily, sometimes in clusters.
Convulsions seizures have been reported only twice in the last year. focal tonic with retained awareness are experienced two - six times daily, sometimes in clusters.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies. The letter states: 'Convulsions seizures have been reported only twice in the last year.' and 'focal tonic with retained awareness are experienced two - six times daily, sometimes in clusters.' Apply the rule to choose the highest frequency across types: 2 per year for convulsions versus 2 to 6 per day for focal events. The higher frequency is 2 to 6 per day. No need to sum as they are different seizure types; we select the more frequent one. Convert wording to allowed format: '2 to 6 per day.'
seizure_frequency_number: focal tonic with retained awareness are experienced two - six times daily, sometimes in clusters.
